meta.section: Endpoint Management — Windows (Phase 2 of 7: Endpoint Security — Antivirus / ASR / Defender for Endpoint)
meta.source: Microsoft Learn (learn.microsoft.com/intune + learn.microsoft.com/defender-endpoint) — portal paths, profile types, and settings grounded via the Microsoft Learn MCP on 2026-07-03.
generalNotes: All of these are Endpoint security policies in the Intune admin center (Endpoint security node). The pattern is the same for each: Create Policy > select Platform = Windows and the named Profile > configure > assign to the Windows device group.

Do them in order: (1) turn on the Defender for Endpoint connector (DefenderForEndpointSettings), (2) onboard devices via the EDR policy (MDE Onboarding), then (3) the Antivirus, ASR Rules, Exploit Protection, and Device Control policies — the last group only reports/works once devices are onboarded.

Roll out ASR Rules and Device Control in AUDIT mode first, review the Defender/ASR reports for false positives, then switch to Block. This is the safe path for the two settings that can actually break a user workflow.
Everything here needs Microsoft Defender for Endpoint Plan 1 (included in Microsoft 365 Business Premium).

requiredSettings.label: Connector
requiredSettings.value: Intune ↔ Microsoft Defender for Endpoint connection = Enabled (established once the MDE service connection is set up)

requiredSettings.label: Connect Windows devices to Defender for Endpoint

steps: Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Microsoft Defender for Endpoint.

Open the Microsoft Defender for Endpoint admin center link if the connection status isn't 'Enabled', complete the connection, then return — the onboarding/offboarding blobs flow into Intune automatically once connected.
Turn on the connection settings (allow MDE to enforce endpoint security configuration; connect Windows devices).
Note: LOW impact / admin-only — this is the bridge every other policy in this phase depends on. Set it first.

requiredSettings.label: Policy type
requiredSettings.value: Endpoint security > Endpoint detection and response (EDR) > profile: Endpoint detection and response

requiredSettings.label: Defender for Endpoint client configuration package type

steps: Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Endpoint detection and response > Create Policy > Windows > profile Endpoint detection and response.
Set 'client configuration package type' = Auto from connector; Sample sharing = All.
Assign to the Windows device group and create. Devices begin onboarding to MDE in the background.
Note: LOW impact / invisible to users — installs the MDE sensor. Depends on the connector above being Enabled.

requiredSettings.label: Policy type
requiredSettings.value: Endpoint security > Antivirus > profile: Microsoft Defender Antivirus

requiredSettings.label: Cloud-delivered protection
requiredSettings.value: Allowed (with sample submission = Send safe samples automatically)

requiredSettings.label: Scan scope
requiredSettings.value: Scan network/mapped drives and removable drives during full scan = Yes; real-time scan direction = monitor all files

requiredSettings.label: PUA protection
requiredSettings.value: On (block potentially unwanted apps); remediation = Quarantine for all severities

steps: Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Antivirus > Create Policy > Windows > profile Microsoft Defender Antivirus.
Enable real-time + behavior monitoring, cloud-delivered protection, network/removable-drive scanning, PUA protection, and set remediation actions to Quarantine.
Assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — users may notice slightly longer scheduled scans; no day-to-day interaction.

requiredSettings.label: Policy type
requiredSettings.value: Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > profile: Attack surface reduction rules

requiredSettings.label: Key rules
requiredSettings.value: Block Office apps creating child processes; block Win32 API calls from Office macros; block credential stealing from lsass; block executable content from email/webmail; block obfuscated scripts; advanced ransomware protection — per the baseline

requiredSettings.label: Initial mode
requiredSettings.value: Audit first, then Block after review

steps: Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > Create Policy > Windows > profile Attack surface reduction rules.
Set the baseline ASR rules to Audit mode initially; assign to a pilot group.
Review the Defender ASR report for legitimate apps that would be blocked, add exclusions as needed, then switch the rules to Block and broaden assignment.
Note (MEDIUM impact): heavy macro users / executable-attachment workflows may hit blocks — audit-first is strongly recommended before enforcing.

requiredSettings.label: Policy type
requiredSettings.value: Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > profile: Exploit Protection

requiredSettings.label: Exploit Protection Settings
requiredSettings.value: Configured — supplied via an exploit protection XML mitigation file (DEP, ASLR, CFG, etc.)

steps: Prepare the exploit-protection XML (export from a reference device via Windows Security > App & browser control, or use the Inforcer baseline XML).
Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > Create Policy > Windows > profile Exploit Protection.
Set 'Exploit Protection Settings' = Configured and upload the XML; assign to the Windows device group.
Note: LOW impact — system-level exploit mitigations; rarely a legitimate app misbehaves, worth watching during rollout.

requiredSettings.label: Policy type
requiredSettings.value: Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > profile: Device Control

requiredSettings.label: Mode
requiredSettings.value: Audit only — monitors/logs USB & removable-media activity, does NOT block

steps: Intune admin center > Endpoint security > Attack surface reduction > Create Policy > Windows > profile Device Control.
Configure the removable-storage / USB rules in Audit (report-only) mode; assign to the Windows device group.
Review the resulting device-control events in the Defender portal.

Note: LOW impact as audit-only (USB keeps working). If ever switched to Block/enforce, impact jumps to HIGH — users would lose USB/removable-media access, so treat that as a separate, communicated change.
